ground_truth.title: Tutoring Pre K-12: Evidence and Resources
ground_truth.main_content:
In North America, millions of students are behind grade level—a number that will only continue to grow in the wake of Covid-19. This reality is particularly troubling as once students are behind, it can be very difficult to catch up. These issues disproportionately harm low-income students and students of color due to racial and economic inequities embedded within the American education system. As schools work to stabilize and adapt in the wake of Covid-19, effective methods to help students catch up are essential. Tutoring—supplemental one-on-one or small group instruction—represents a proven and agreed-upon strategy to combat Covid-19 learning loss.

The Evidence: J-PAL North America's Tutoring Evidence Review summarizes the evidence from a meta-analysis of 96 randomized evaluations of tutoring programs. Key findings include: tutoring programs consistently lead to significant improvements in learning outcomes; tutoring programs led by teachers or professional tutors are generally more effective than programs using nonprofessional or parent tutors; effects tend to be strongest among students in earlier grades; reading tutoring tends to be relatively more effective for students in preschool through first grade while math tutoring tends to be more effective for students in second through fifth grade; and tutoring programs conducted during school tend to have larger impacts than those conducted after school.

Tutoring In Action: One tutoring model that consistently generates significant learning outcomes is Saga Education. An evaluation of Saga's program in Chicago Public Schools found that tutoring helped students learn an extra one to two years' worth of math beyond what their peers learned in an academic year. A 2021 randomized evaluation found that these gains persisted a year or two after the tutoring occurred.

Policy Recommendations: Tutoring sessions should occur at schools either during the school day or immediately before or after; sessions should occur a minimum of three sessions per week; students should meet with a consistent paid and trained tutor who is supported by ongoing oversight and coaching; sessions should be informed by data; and materials should be aligned with research and state standards.

Implementation Support in California: In 2020, J-PAL North America staff worked with the California governor's office, leading to the inclusion of $460 million for hiring paraprofessional tutors into Assembly Bill 86.

Ongoing Projects include Khoaching with Khan Academy, which leverages computer-assisted learning, teachers, and parents to facilitate personalized learning at scale, and a study on Understanding the importance of summer learning in a pandemic.
with: In North America, millions of students are behind grade level—a number that will only continue to grow in the wake of Covid-19.
Tutoring programs led by teachers or professional tutors—tutors who are paid and trained—are generally more effective than programs that used nonprofessional (volunteer) or parent tutors.

The effects of tutoring programs tend to be strongest among students in earlier grades, though a smaller set of programs at the secondary level were also found to be effective at improving learning outcomes.
Tutoring sessions should occur at schools either during the school day or immediately before or after the school day.
An evaluation of Saga's program in Chicago Public Schools found that Saga tutoring helped students learn an extra one to two years' worth of math beyond what their peers learned in an academic year.
